headlines.title: SF Mayor Daniel Lurie pushes Muni funding measures as deadline nears
headlines.description: San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie was out Wednesday morning gathering signatures to help two transit measures make the November ballot. Lurie was near the Market and Castro Muni stop, encouraging people to support the “Stronger Muni for All” measure and a regional proposal known as “Connect Bay Area.” Both measures aim to provide long-term funding for transit. The local measure would fund Muni, while the regional measure would support agencies like BART and Caltrain. The mayor said Muni does not receive state funding and is relying on a proposed parcel tax. That would cost renters about $65 a year and most homeowners about $129 annually. “It is a price, so I’m not discounting that, but it is something we have to pay,” Lurie said. Meanwhile, the city is increasing enforcement on Muni, adding about 30% more fare inspectors. The agency recently reported its highest ridership since the start of the pandemic. Regionally, leaders are also backing a sales tax measure that could generate about $980 million a year to help avoid major service cuts. That regional measure needs about 186,000 valid signatures to qualify for the November ballot. San Francisco 14 hours ago San Francisco Apr 28 Getting back on track: Muni records highest ridership since pandemic low

headlines.title: Outside Lands weddings return with new $15,000 experience
headlines.description: Outside Lands is returning to Golden Gate Park in San Francisco in a few months, and couples once again will be able to get married at the festival. For a third year, organizers are offering wedding ceremonies starting at about $450. The package includes an officiant and a photographer, but spots are limited. A new premium option is also available this year. Couples can hold a ceremony on the Lands End main stage for just under $16,000. That package includes a two-hour reception for up to 20 guests, along with flowers, cupcakes and a champagne toast. Tickets for guests are not included. San Francisco Mar 3 San Francisco Aug 10, 2025 Outside Lands brings more music, food, and fog for its 17th year

headlines.title: OpenAI trial live updates: Shivon Zilis takes the stand
headlines.description: What to Know Elon Musk has filed a $134 billion lawsuit against Sam Altman and his top lieutenant, Greg Brockman, along with Microsoft over its investments in OpenAI, in 2024. Musk claims OpenAI violated its original mission to operate as a nonprofit focused on developing artificial intelligence for the benefit of humanity. OpenAI has brushed off Musk’s allegations as a case of sour grapes aimed at undercutting its rapid growth and bolstering Musk’s own xAI, which he launched in 2023 as a competitor. The nine-person jury was selected on April 27. The trial is being held in Oakland federal court and scheduled to continue through late May. Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers excused Musk from the witness stand Thursday, but he may be called back later. The landmark OpenAI trial continues Wednesday a day after the company’s president, Greg Brockman, finished his testimony. Brockman was called to the stand on Monday and Tuesday to discuss how OpenAI was formed and his relationship with Elon Musk, who has filed a $134 billion lawsuit against him and OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, along with Microsoft over its investments in the company. The OpenAI president on Tuesday testified he feared Musk would “physically attack” him during a tense confrontation following a disagreement about potential equity shares. Brockman on Monday was hammered by Musk’s attorney about his money and “conflict of interest” investing. Brockman during testimony also disclosed his stake in OpenAI is worth $30 billion. The trial centers on the 2015 birth of the ChatGPT maker as a nonprofit startup primarily funded by Musk before evolving into a capitalistic venture now valued at $852 billion. It pits the world’s richest person against Altman, a fellow OpenAI co-founder he accuses of betraying promises to keep the company as a nonprofit dedicated to humanity’s benefit. Musk last week spent three days on the stand before he was excused by the judge. While the Tesla CEO was not in court Monday, he could be recalled to stand again in the trial. NBC Bay Area business and tech reporter Scott Budman is at the federal courthouse in Oakland and providing live updates below.
headlines.source: Scott Budman, Kristofer Noceda, Joel Soria and Brendan Weber

headlines.title: Candidates for California governor tangle in testy debate with mail voting already underway
headlines.description: Seven candidates who want to be California’s next governor traded sharp attacks Tuesday in a wide-ranging debate that touched on issues from gas prices to raising taxes to healthcare in a contest that has no clear leader. The televised debate came as mail voting was already underway in advance of a primary election that ends June 2. It was clear from the testy tone that candidates viewed the matchup as a critical juncture, with a national audience watching as it aired on CNN. The candidates are vying to replace Democratic Gov. Gavin Newsom, who is barred by law from seeking a third term, to lead the nation’s most populous state and one of the world’s largest economies. During the two-hour showdown, Republicans Steve Hilton, a conservative commentator, and Riverside County Sheriff Chad Bianco said Democrats have made life worse for California’s nearly 40 million residents during more than 15 years in the governor’s office and that it’s time for a change. The Democrats — former state Attorney General Xavier Becerra, former U.S. Rep. Katie Porter,billionaire Tom Steyer, San Jose Mayor Matt Mahan and former Los Angeles Mayor Antonio Villaraigosa — tried to distinguish themselves on policy and said President Donald Trump is California’s biggest threat. An overarching issue was the punishing cost of living in California and how it might be eased, underscoring deep partisan divides. The average gas price was more than $6 per gallon in California on Tuesday, according to AAA. Becerra was among those who argued Trump and the war in Iran are to blame for rapidly rising costs at the pump. Hilton and Bianco noted gas has long been much more expensive in California compared to other states due to taxes and regulations. But when Hilton said he could reduce gas prices to $3 a gallon, Mahan fired back, “You’re lying to people.” “Donald Trump is the president in all the other states in America where the cost of living is way lower than in California,” responded Hilton, who has Trump’s endorsement. “Boys, boys, enough with the bickering,” Porter, the only woman on stage, interjected at one point. The contest is unfolding as California struggles with a long-running homeless crisis, wildfire insurance shortages, projected budget shortfalls and housing costs that are out of reach for many working-class families. Voters, meanwhile, are saddled with growing everyday bills for groceries, utilities and gas. var pymParent = new pym.Parent('gov-cands', 'https://visuals.otsops.com/projects/ca-gov-cands-2026/index.html', {title: '', parenturlparam: '', parenturlvalue: ''}); Trump — who is widely unpopular in California outside his conservative base — was frequently denounced by Democrats, while Republicans said he was being used as a convenient scapegoat for the failures of the state’s Democratic leadership. “Yes, I’m going to repeat Donald Trump as often as I have to because he’s the real menace that we have in California,” Becerra said. “Donald Trump sucks,” Porter said bluntly. The candidates sparred over tax policy, with only Steyer saying he supports a one-time tax on billionaires that’s likely to be on the ballot this November. He said it wasn’t the only tax increase he’d seek. Porter, meanwhile, said the tax was too narrow and wouldn’t make a real change. Mahan, who wants to suspend the state’s gas tax, said his fellow Democrats have focused too much on expanding government rather than making it work better. Hilton pledged to make people’s first $100,000 free of income tax. Candidates went on the attack against rivals Beyond policy, the candidates looked for openings to knock down their rivals. Becerra was peppered with questions about the federal indictment of his chief of staff and others, including a former top Newsom aide, alleging they stole campaign money from him. The indictment alleges the scheme was designed to pad the salary of Becerra’s chief of staff when Becerra was serving as the federal health secretary. Several people involved have pleaded guilty. Becerra has not been implicated or indicted, but Villaraigosa and Hilton used the incident to question his judgment and suggest he may have been involved. Hilton said that, if elected, he would even push the state attorney general to investigate Becerra. “If I had been involved, the U.S. attorney would have had me in that indictment. I was not involved,” Becerra said. Bianco faced questions about his decision to seize more than half a million ballots in Riverside County to investigate claims of election fraud that local officials say are unfounded. The state Supreme Court ordered his investigation halted. Porter was asked about a new ad in which she refers in jest to an embarrassing video that surfaced last year showing the former House member loudly berating a staffer. Porter said it showed “I’m able to take responsibility.” Decision 2026 May 5 San Francisco Apr 22 She added, “If these boys bullying and bickering hasn’t been enough to raise questions about their temperament, I would really challenge that.” Hilton, meanwhile, refused to answer when pressed about whether Trump lost the 2020 presidential election. California puts all candidates on a single ballot and the two with the most votes go on to the November general election, regardless of party. Democrats have worried that their crowded field could result in two Republicans advancing, which would be a historic calamity for the party. The GOP has not won a statewide election in California in two decades, and registered Democrats outnumber Republicans about 2-to-1 statewide. ___ Austin reported from Sacramento, California.
headlines.source: Michael R. Blood | The Associated Press and Sophie Austin | The Associated Press

headlines.title: With ‘golden ticket,' wartime travel refunds should be easy. But are they?
headlines.description: North Bay accountant Leslie Russo is a bit of a jet setter. “I try to take two trips a year,” she said. “Typically, I’ll go in Spring and Fall.” Leslie’s spring trip, to Korea, went off without a hitch. But her fall trip hit a roadblock. Her destination was Egypt and 10 days sightseeing at many “treasures,” according to the brochure. Leslie booked a tour with a South Lake Tahoe outfit called Snow More Ski and Board Club. She started making payments in March of 2025. “I paid $3,900 in three different deposits,” she said. “And then, this war broke out in the Middle East and I was getting a little nervous. And I decided I’m going to cancel the trip.” Canceling a trip is sometimes a pain– costly, too. So, how about during war time? “There has been a general consensus that airlines, hotels, and travel providers are trying to be as accommodating as possible,” said Katy Nastro with Going.com. Nastro says many travel companies are offering special flexibility: wartime fee waivers that let you change or refund a trip — even if your booking is non-changeable or non-refundable. She calls the waivers a ‘golden ticket.’ “We like to call it a ‘golden ticket’ scenario because it’s a Charlie and the Chocolate Factory moment. You have the keys to make changes,” without normal penalties and fees. Leslie used her ‘golden ticket.’ First: with her flight. “I called the airline,” Leslie said. “They gave me my money back in three days.” Next, Leslie tried to cancel the land portion of her Egypt trip. Not so ‘golden.’ She says she told Snow More Ski and Board Club in March that she wasn’t going on the October trip. Her paperwork says a $300 cancellation fee kicks in when you cancel within 159 days of a trip. Leslie was cancelling more than 200 days before the Egypt trip. But, she said Snow More Ski and Board Club wanted to deduct $1,900 from her refund. “It was a very fair, clear cancellation policy,” she said. “But now that I actually want to cancel… nada, nunca, not happening!” Leslie refused to accept the unwritten deduction. She contacted NBC Bay Area Responds. “I went to Chris Chmura, because I watch you on TV all the time. And I thought, maybe this is the way I have to go,” she said. Leslie was optimistic about our intervention. “Thank you for taking it on. I have high hopes,” she said. Our team then contacted Snow More Ski and Board Club president Cheryl Lawson several times over several weeks. When she responded, she said: “This matter has been resolved. While it is surprising that such a minor issue would be of interest for a broadcast, we have taken the necessary steps to address Ms. Russo’s concern.” Leslie says the club then mailed her a check. But it was $1,900 short. The letter with it included language that says, “Cancellations between June 15, 2025 – June 15, 2026 – will forfeit the initial deposit of $400 and an additional $1,500 per person.” That’s $1,900 in total. “Where was that $1,900 in your contract,” I asked Leslie. “Nowhere,” she said. “Chris, you saw the contract. It was nowhere.” The club’s website still advertises the Egypt tour with no mention of the $1,900 penalty. We pressed the Club about that. No response. So, we shared Leslie’s case with California’s Travel Consumer Restitution Corporation. It’s a small, state-sanctioned organization with a big pot of money — more than $3.6M — to reimburse consumers when registered travel sellers, like the club, go belly up or there’s a “material failure” to deliver their services. Very few people use the TCRC. Only 44 people filed claims in a full program year, its statistics show. The TCRC recommended Leslie file an official consumer complaint. She’s going to. “I want to get my money back,” she said. We’ll keep you posted. If you ever use a travel agent or travel website, it’s really important to verify they are registered in California with both the California Attorney General and the TCRC. You can check the agent directory online at tcrcinfo.org. That’s the only way to ensure you’re getting the protection of the consumer restitution fund should your travel agent drop the ball.

headlines.title: Detectives investigate deadly shooting in San Jose
headlines.description: Police in San Jose are investigating a fatal weekend shooting, the department said Tuesday. On Saturday just before 1 a.m., officers were sent to the 1000 block of Summerside Drive after receiving a report of a possible shooting. Police arrived and found a man lying in the driveway of an apartment complex suffering from at least one gunshot wound. He was taken to the hospital, where he died. The suspect fled the scene before police arrived, the department said. Homicide detectives responded to the scene and are investigating the shooting. The motive and circumstances surrounding the event remain under investigation. Anyone with information is asked to contact Det. Sgt. Varela at 3638@sanjoseca.gov or Det. Montoya at 3644@sanjoseca.gov, or call (408) 277-5283.

headlines.title: Former Concord immigration judge sues Trump administration
headlines.description: A former federal immigration judge who served in Concord is now suing the Trump administration. Kyra Lilien was appointed to the bench in July 2023. According to her lawsuit, new immigration judges serve a two-year probationary period before being converted to permanent positions. She claims she and six other female judges at the Concord court were advised last year that they wouldn’t be getting permanent positions. At the same time, Lilien claims four white male immigration judges were retained or had their positions made permanent. Lilien believes she was fired because she is an older female Democrat with a history of advocating for immigrants. Immigration Jul 18, 2025 Investigative Unit Oct 20, 2025 Former judges give inside look at immigration court upheaval

headlines.title: South Bay tattoo artist arrested for alleged sexual assaults
headlines.description: A South Bay tattoo artist has been taken into custody on suspicion of sexually assaulting at least two women, Milpitas police announced Tuesday. Tai To, 45, of San Jose allegedly sexually assaulted one woman during a tattoo appointment at an undisclosed location in Milpitas, according to police. The woman reported the alleged assault to police in March. Detectives took on the case and learned of a second woman who allegedly was sexually assaulted by To during “previous interactions,” police said. To was arrested in San Jose on April 30, police said. He was booked into jail for felony sexual assault. Police did not provide the name of the tattoo shop. Anyone with information about the cases or potential victims are asked to contact Milpitas police.

headlines.title: Driver arrested after car crashes into San Jose home
headlines.description: A driver was arrested after a car crashed into a home Tuesday night in San Jose. Officers responded to the 900 block of North 17th Street on a report of a vehicle into a house. When they arrived, they learned the vehicle had collided with a light pole before striking the home’s carport. Two juveniles who were in the backyard at the time suffered non-life-threatening injuries. Police said the adult male driver was believed to be under the influence. He was taken to a local hospital for evaluation and later booked into the Santa Clara County jail on suspicion of DUI.

headlines.title: Overturned big rig blocked northbound Highway 101 lanes in Gilroy
headlines.description: An overturned semi-truck blocked lanes Wednesday morning on northbound Highway 101 in Gilroy. At least two lanes were closed after the truck, which was carrying asphalt, overturned just before midnight. The cause of the crash is under investigation. No injuries were reported. The truck has been lifted upright, and all lanes have since reopened.

headlines.title: Vigil held at SFSU for human rights activist killed in Philippines
headlines.description: Friends, family and former classmates are mourning the death of San Francisco State University alum Lyle Prijoles, a human rights activist killed alongside 18 others in the Philippines last month. A community vigil was held Tuesday night at SFSU to honor Prijoles and the other victims. Pete Suratos reports from the university. Watch his story in the video above.

headlines.title: Manhunt underway after shooting near Cinco de Mayo festival in Santa Rosa
headlines.description: Police in Santa Rosa are investigating a shooting on Cinco de Mayo and have offered a reward in the case, the department said Tuesday. Just before 8:30 p.m., officers assigned to Cinco de Mayo festivities in the area of Sebastopol Road and Avalon Avenue heard gunshots coming from the area of Dutton Avenue. They soon spotted a man in his early 20s running toward them with a gunshot wound. An ambulance was called and the man is in stable condition at the hospital, the department said. Investigators learned that shortly before the shots were fired, there was an altercation in the parking lot of the 400 block of Sebastopol Road. A suspect drew a gun and fired several rounds, at least one of which struck the victim. The suspect then fled from the area and was described as a man in his twenties wearing a grey sweatshirt. Anyone who witnessed the shooting or has information that would assist with the investigation is encouraged to contact the Santa Rosa Police Department through its online tip line at www.srcity.org/CrimeTips. Information can also be given by contacting Gang Crime Investigations detectives at (707) 543-3670. A reward of up to $2,500 is being offered by the Sonoma County Alliance Community Engagement and Safety Rewards Fund. The reward is for information leading to the arrest of the suspect or suspects involved in this shooting.
